industries.name: Fundamental Nuclear & Atomic Research
uses: Named after Hesse (Hassia in Latin), the German state where GSI is located. Produced by bombarding lead-208 with iron-58 ions. The gas-phase chemistry of HsO4 — performed by a GSI/PSI collaboration — is the heaviest element chemistry ever studied directly, confirming hassium as a heavier osmium analog.
Longest-lived known isotope: 270Hs, t½ = 10 seconds. First synthesized: 1984, GSI Darmstadt (Münzenberg, Hofmann group).

Research probes the limits of nuclear stability, tests relativistic quantum chemistry predictions in superheavy atoms, and searches for the theoretical island of stability predicted around Z=114, N=184.
All production requires specialized particle accelerators and isotope reactor facilities — this element cannot be made by any other means.

companies.name: GSI Helmholtz Centre
companies.use: Discovered element 108 in 1984 using the SHIP separator with lead-208 + iron-58 cold fusion. Led the landmark HsO4 gas-phase chemistry experiment in 2002 in collaboration with PSI, demonstrating that hassium forms volatile tetroxide just like osmium — the heaviest chemical property ever directly measured.

companies.name: Paul Scherrer Institute (PSI)
companies.use: Key partner in the HsO4 chemistry experiment — the most technically demanding chemical study of any element. PSI's thermochromatography detector array identified and measured the adsorption properties of individual HsO4 molecules produced at a rate of a few atoms per hour.

companies.name: Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory
companies.use: Contributed nuclear spectroscopy data and detection techniques for hassium characterization. LBNL's expertise in actinide and transactinide nuclear physics provided cross-checks on GSI's decay chain assignments.
